summary: Italy-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2018 after a hack-related crisis.

summary: Cryptocurrency exchange publicly treated as dead after a late-December 2020 hack crisis, with later formal closure announced in January 2021.
notes: HEI uses the 2020-12-27 public dead-side marker from Cryptowisser rather than the later 2021-01-18 formal closure announcement. Death reason is mapped to hack because public reporting and the later closure announcement both tied the shutdown to the December 2020 attack. Public sources disagree on origin and establishment details; HEI retains Seychelles from the exchange review and 2013-01-01 as a conservative launch marker from exchange-statistics coverage.

summary: Australian cryptocurrency exchange launched in March 2016 that shut down on 2019-10-15 after announcing a purely business-driven closure.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2019-10-15 closure update. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown because public exchange-status coverage described the decision as purely business driven.

summary: Canada-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2018 after a scam or rug-pull style failure.
notes: Public reporting conflicts on whether the terminal crisis is better characterized as a hack or an exit scam.

summary: Taiwan-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2020 after a voluntary shutdown.
notes: Reuters announced the platform launch in September 2017, but the live platform launch reference used here is 2017-12-18. Shutdown marker uses the 2020-01-10 customer notice.

summary: Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2020 after a hack-related crisis.
notes: Public coverage indicates the venue was only a few months old by February 2020. The operational end marker uses the published refund deadline in May 2020.

summary: Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2018 after regulatory or enforcement action.
notes: Launch uses the September 2017 rebrand/launch announcement. Death marker uses the late-November 2018 domain-loss point as an operational end marker; exact final shutdown timing remains contested.

summary: Thailand-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2019 after a voluntary shutdown.
notes: Company statements emphasized closure to pursue other opportunities; some secondary profiles also mention regulatory friction.

summary: Singapore-based centralized exchange that appears inactive, with closure details that remain only partially verified.
notes: COSS was taken offline on 2020-01-07 and later described acquisition negotiations and a relaunch plan. Because the record is too ambiguous for a clean dead-side classification, it is moved to inactive pending deeper verification.

summary: Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2014 after an insolvency-related collapse.
notes: Launch uses repeated October 2011 references. Operational end marker uses the March 2014 withdrawal freeze and account-freeze period, which followed earlier hacks and a reserve crisis.

summary: India-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2014 after a scam or rug-pull style failure.
Centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2018 after a scam or rug-pull style failure.
notes: Launch remains approximate because strong primary founding records are still missing. Operational end marker uses the point when users widely reported trading and withdrawals disabled on 2018-01-05.

summary: United Kingdom-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2014 after a hack-related crisis.
notes: Launch uses a specific February 2014 launch reference. Death marker uses 2014-10-14 when Moolah announced MintPal had been shut down while preparing bankruptcy; later fallout continued after that date.

summary: Canada-based centralized exchange that ceased operations in 2015 after a voluntary shutdown.
notes: Launch uses October 2013 reporting on the exchange going live. Closure date and reason come from the January 2015 shutdown announcement specifying a voluntary end rather than insolvency or theft.

summary: Finnish peer-to-peer bitcoin marketplace that halted trading in February 2023 after announcing closure during the crypto winter.
notes: Original domain still carries an official closure notice; archive-first display remains appropriate for dead-side history.

summary: New York bitcoin exchange founded in 2012 and fully absorbed into Kraken in January 2016.
notes: Death date uses Kraken's customer-transfer date rather than the initial acquisition announcement date.

summary: Canadian bitcoin exchange founded in 2011 and folded into Kraken in January 2016.
notes: Death date uses Kraken's customer-transfer date rather than the initial acquisition announcement date.

summary: European exchange brand that rebranded from BitBay to Zonda on 2021-11-08.
notes: Future successor handling should avoid double-counting Zonda and zondacrypto as separate entities without lineage review.

summary: LINE-backed exchange that announced closure in November 2022 and ended customer withdrawals by 2023-03-31.
notes: Entity death date uses the end of withdrawals and service termination rather than the announcement date.

summary: Claims-and-derivatives trading platform launched from the CoinFLEX rebrand and shut down in February 2024.
notes: Shutdown date is supported by strong reporting, but this retained batch does not include a direct official closure notice; future predecessor and successor review should decide whether CoinFLEX and OPNX remain separate HEI entities or linked lineage records.

summary: South Korean centralized exchange that halted trading and entered bankruptcy proceedings after a second 2017 hack.
notes: Alias handling preserves the earlier Yapizon identity without treating it as a separate HEI entity. The retained death marker follows same-day shutdown and bankruptcy reporting after the second 2017 hack.

summary: Hong Kong centralized exchange that ceased operations in March 2019 after a court-ordered wind-up and liquidation process.
notes: Operational stop follows the 2019-03-13 winding-up order. Liquidators were appointed on 2019-03-20 and that later date remains documented in evidence.

summary: Indian centralized exchange that permanently disabled trading on 2019-06-27 amid regulatory uncertainty and disruption.
notes: The retained death marker follows Koinex's 2019-06-27 permanent trading shutdown announcement. Launch date remains year-level approximate pending stronger founding evidence.

summary: United Kingdom bitcoin exchange brand whose customer base and domains were acquired by CoinCorner in October 2021.
notes: Official successor-side support materials state that CoinCorner acquired Coinfloor in October 2021 and migrated or closed legacy accounts.

summary: Dutch bitcoin exchange founded in 2013 and folded into Kraken following a June 2016 acquisition and account transfer.
notes: Entity death_date uses the customer-transfer completion date after Kraken's 2016-06-28 acquisition announcement.

summary: South Korean centralized exchange that began its shutdown process in April 2019 and required users to withdraw assets by the end of the month.
notes: Closure is supported by multiple contemporaneous reports quoting the official site notice. Cause remains mixed across declining volume, regulatory background, and business decisions, so death_reason stays unknown.

summary: Ukraine-linked centralized exchange that announced its shutdown on 2019-01-28 and gave users a 30 day withdrawal window.
notes: Shutdown language cited insufficient liquidity, but this batch keeps death_reason as unknown. A 2025 official retrospective Medium post confirms that a shutdown announcement was issued in January 2019.

summary: Altcoin-focused exchange that announced a shutdown in late September 2019 and ceased trading and API access on 2019-10-07.
notes: The closure schedule is well supported, but the cause is kept as unknown to avoid overcommitting on business-decision language preserved through third-party mirrors and listings.

summary: Vancouver-based centralized exchange that entered court-supervised receivership in November 2019 after customer-access failures and large asset shortfalls.
notes: The shutdown path involves receivership, asset shortfalls, and later fraud findings, so the retained death_reason remains unknown.

summary: Italian centralized exchange that interrupted operations in February 2023 due to liquidity problems and later entered judicial liquidation in Milan.
notes: Operational death marker uses the 2023-02-17 interruption of platform operations. Later court-supervised insolvency and judicial liquidation are documented separately in evidence.

summary: Austrian virtual-world and bitcoin exchange that announced an end of service in late 2019 and permanently closed on 2020-01-06.
notes: Closure timing is clear, but the detailed reason is not strongly primary-sourced in this pass; status remains dead with a conservative voluntary shutdown classification.

summary: Polish exchange that went offline in October 2016 after reporting external interference and major asset losses.
notes: Uses the October 2016 operational stop. Investigation context is preserved in evidence.

summary: Early U.S. bitcoin exchange that shut down in 2012, relaunched, then halted trading again in 2013 because of banking and regulatory issues.
notes: The final operational end uses the August 2013 halt rather than the earlier 2012 shutdown.

summary: U.S. bitcoin exchange whose operators were charged for running an unlawful exchange and money-laundering scheme.
notes: Uses the July 2015 enforcement-side operational end marker.

summary: Early UK bitcoin exchange that progressively shut down markets in 2012 and ceased operations after banking problems and shrinking volume.
notes: Uses the 2012-12-19 GBP market closure as the terminal marker.

summary: Regional Binance fiat surface for Ugandan shillings that shut down in November 2020 as Binance consolidated users onto Binance.com.
notes: Lineage-sensitive regional surface. Keep separate from Binance.com.

summary: Polish exchange that suffered catastrophic wallet data loss in July 2011 and was acquired by Mt. Gox in August 2011.
notes: This record belongs on an acquired-lineage track with Mt. Gox as successor.

summary: Canadian exchange that announced an immediate shutdown in March 2016 after an alleged hack and wallet deficiency.
notes: Uses the exchange's immediate shutdown notice as the death marker.

summary: Chinese exchange that announced the closure of trading operations during the 2017 domestic crackdown.

summary: Regional Binance-branded fiat exchange that shut down at the end of November 2020 after Binance expanded fiat access on its main platform.

summary: Singapore-local Binance affiliate that wound down and closed after the operator withdrew its licensing effort in the country.
notes: Lineage-sensitive regional surface. Keep separate from Binance.com.

summary: BitShares-based decentralized exchange that announced closure in December 2019, citing market conditions and increasing regulation pressure.

summary: Polish cryptocurrency exchange that shut down in April 2019 after earlier KNF warning-list action, with later reporting alleging customer-fund losses.

summary: Ireland-registered cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2016 that disappeared in June 2019 after withdrawal problems and was publicly treated as an exit-scam style dead exchange.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2019-06-27 public inactive / scam-style marker from Cryptowisser rather than the earlier 2019-06-17 disappearance date. Death reason is mapped to scam_rug because public reporting and the exchange review both describe classic exit-scam characteristics after blocked withdrawals and deletion of the website and social accounts.

summary: China-based exchange later transitioned from Bter.com to Gate.io during the 2017 China regulatory crackdown.
notes: Treat as lineage-sensitive rebrand record to Gate.io. The 2015 hack is a major historical event but not the terminal state.

summary: Polish cryptocurrency exchange that later shut down after earlier KNF regulatory action and was marked closed by February 2019.

summary: United Kingdom cryptocurrency exchange that was later marked inactive and publicly described as having closed operations by 2019-04-01.

summary: Russian cryptocurrency exchange that became inaccessible by late June 2019 and was subsequently marked dead, although some later status pages still described it as suspended for reorganization.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2019-06-27 dead-side update, while acknowledging later status-page ambiguity about reorganization.

summary: Advanced trading surface under Coinbase that was replaced by Coinbase Advanced, with customer history access ending after December 2023.
notes: Lineage-sensitive product transition. After balance migration, customers could no longer deposit, withdraw, or trade on Coinbase Pro, and transaction history remained accessible only until the end of 2023.

summary: China-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2013 that was publicly marked inaccessible and dead by mid-June 2019.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2019-06-12 dead-side update.

summary: Cryptocurrency exchange originally from Brazil that was later described as inaccessible and dead by March 2023 after earlier relocations to Malta and Portugal.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Bleutrade was originally from Brazil, later moved to Malta in 2018 and Portugal in 2020, and was marked dead after the site was inaccessible on 2023-03-23.

summary: China-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly marked inaccessible and dead on 2021-07-15.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2021-07-15 dead-side update.

summary: Canada-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly described as closed in early 2025, with user inquiries ending on 2025-04-01.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2025-04-01 user-inquiry end date from the public closure update.

summary: Australian cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly described as inaccessible and dead by 2021-07-15.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2021-07-15 dead-side update.

summary: Polish cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly described as inaccessible and dead by 2021-11-11.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2021-11-11 dead-side update.

summary: Seychelles-registered cryptocurrency derivatives exchange that became unusable by 2023-01-11 and was marked dead after posting a message about developing a new platform.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2023-01-11 dead-side update. The 'new platform' notice is treated as context, not proof of a successful successor.

summary: Russian cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly described as dead by 2019-07-18 after volume-reporting ceased, the site presented an invalid certificate, and trading activity appeared stale.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Cryptowisser review update uses 2019-07-18 as the dead-side marker, while Cryptowisser graveyard shows 2019-07-17 with business reasons.

summary: Crypto exchange branded as ZT that was publicly described as inaccessible and dead by 2024-09-18, with CoinMarketCap later showing that withdrawals and trading were suspended.

summary: Cryptocurrency exchange branded as AAX that suspended withdrawals in November 2022 and was publicly marked dead by 2023-01-11.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2023-01-11 dead-side update. Source coverage shows a 2022-11-13 suspension first, then a later dead-side marker.

summary: Greece-linked non-custodial exchange or broker-style swap platform that was publicly described as inaccessible and dead by 2024-09-18.
notes: Treat cause conservatively as unknown. Terminal marker uses the 2024-09-18 dead-side update. Launch year is 2019, recorded conservatively as 2019-01-01.

summary: BNB Chain-born decentralized exchange and DeFi protocol family launched in 2020 and still active as a multichain DEX stack under the PancakeSwap brand.

summary: Bitcoin-focused peer-to-peer decentralized exchange network that went into production in 2016 and remains active through Bisq 1 and Bisq 2.
notes: Entity-level umbrella record. Launch marker uses the documented production date. Bisq 1 and Bisq 2 are not yet split into separate protocol or product records.

summary: Dutch cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly marked closed on 2020-09-01 after citing Dutch registration requirements and operating-cost pressure.
notes: Cause stays conservative as unknown. Public closure text references Dutch registration requirements and economic infeasibility, but HEI does not hard-classify this beyond the terminal closure marker.

summary: Brazil-linked cryptocurrency exchange associated with XRP trading that is now permanently closed according to public exchange-status sources.
notes: Cause stays conservative as unknown. Public handling relies on exchange-status coverage stating the platform is permanently closed and that a closure notice was shown on the website.

summary: Polish cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly marked dead on 2021-07-15 after its website became inaccessible with no preceding maintenance or migration notice.
notes: Cause stays conservative as unknown. Public handling relies on the dead-side status update tied to site inaccessibility on 2021-07-15.

summary: Exchange-like cryptocurrency swap service reachable via eXch.cx that was seized and shut down by German authorities on 2025-04-30 after investigators alleged money laundering and operation of a criminal online trading platform.
notes: Current HEI scope treats exchange-like swap services as in-scope. The terminal marker uses the 2025-04-30 infrastructure seizure and shutdown by German authorities, which preempted the operators' own planned 2025-05-01 closure.

summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2014 that was publicly reported bankrupt on 2021-01-12 and is no longer operating.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-01-12 bankruptcy update. Death reason is set to insolvency based on the bankruptcy filing and dead-side treatment.

summary: Seychelles-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly treated as dead on 2023-01-03 after disappearing from normal operation.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2023-01-03 graveyard marker. Cause stays conservative as unknown even though public status coverage characterizes the exchange as having disappeared.

summary: Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2017 that was publicly treated as closed on 2021-01-01 amid regulation-linked shutdown handling.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-01-01 dead-side update. Death reason is set to regulation in line with public exchange-status coverage.

summary: Estonia-linked cryptocurrency exchange that was publicly treated as dead on 2021-01-01 for business-related shutdown reasons.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-01-01 graveyard marker. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown from business-reasons style closure handling.

summary: South Korean cryptocurrency exchange launched in March 2018 that closed all exchange services on 2021-03-22 and was publicly treated as a business-reasons shutdown.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-03-22 dead-side update. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown because public exchange-status coverage says no specific reason was given and assumes business reasons.

summary: Slovakia-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2019 that paused operations on 2021-04-22 after a 2020 hack crisis and was subsequently treated as dead.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-04-22 paused-operations marker. Death reason is mapped to hack because the shutdown followed the September 2020 hot-wallet compromise, although later bankruptcy context also exists.

summary: Hong Kong-based cryptocurrency exchange active since 2017 that was publicly marked dead in late 2021 after prolonged financial troubles and business-driven closure handling.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2021-12-02 public dead-side update. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown from business-reasons closure handling. Cryptowisser's graveyard also places Coinsuper in the 2021 business-reasons cohort.

summary: United States-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2018 that announced closure in November 2020 and was publicly treated as dead by the end of 2020 under business-reasons shutdown handling.
notes: Cryptowisser review preserves a 2020-11-18 closing-down update, while Cryptowisser graveyard places MercuriEx in the 2020-12-31 business-reasons dead-side cohort. Some SurfTheWeb materials say the exchange was developed or operational since late 2017, but HEI retains 2018-01-01 as the conservative public launch marker.

summary: United Kingdom-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in 2015 that stopped providing services on 2020-03-30 and was subsequently treated as dead.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2020-03-30 closure update. Public review coverage confirms United Kingdom domicile and 2015 launch, while the preserved closure message cites technical problems and a final withdrawal deadline but does not support a cleaner cause classification than unknown.

summary: Estonia-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in August 2019 that announced closure of its centralized exchange on 2024-12-18 for economic reasons and was subsequently treated as dead.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2024-12-18 public dead-side marker. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown because the official closure notice said the centralized exchange was closing solely for economic reasons, while some token utility continued on Nominswap.

summary: Isle of Man-based cryptocurrency exchange that publicly closed its retail service on 2023-01-24 in order to focus on B2B operations and was subsequently treated as dead-side.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2023-01-24 public closure marker. Death reason is mapped to voluntary_shutdown because Coindirect announced that it was closing its retail service to focus on B2B business. Public review coverage describes the exchange as from Isle of Man and active since 2017, while the preserved launch press release shows a January 2018 live launch; HEI uses 2018-01-01 as the conservative public launch marker.

summary: Hong Kong-based cryptocurrency exchange active since May 2018 that was marked scam and moved to dead-side on 2022-08-17 after widespread withdrawal and account-blocking complaints.
notes: Terminal handling uses the 2022-08-17 public dead-side marker. Death reason is mapped to scam_rug because public review coverage states that the exchange was marked as a scam after numerous withdrawal failures and blocked-account reports. CoinMarketCap later displayed a warning that exchange activities were suspended.

summary: Singapore-based cryptocurrency exchange launched in January 2018 that was publicly marked as a scam and moved to dead-side handling on 2019-11-01 after widespread accusations from users.
